identified_error: The paper’s central methodological/statistical error is that it analyzed a 4-arm, repeated-measures randomized trial with inappropriate pairwise independent t-tests, treating observations as if they were independent and apparently inferring treatment efficacy largely from within-group pre/post significance rather than from a valid group-by-time comparison. Each participant was measured repeatedly at weeks 0, 4, 8, and 12, so the data are correlated within person; independent t-tests are not valid for this design. In addition, the correct primary question in an RCT is whether change over time differs between ACV and placebo, not whether ACV groups change from their own baseline while placebo does not. This is a classic wrong-unit/wrong-test error for longitudinal trial data.
why_fatal: Because the statistical model is wrong, the reported p-values and significance claims cannot be trusted. Repeated measurements on the same subjects violate the independence assumption of the independent t-test, which can distort standard errors and inflate false-positive findings. More importantly, showing that an ACV group changed significantly from baseline does not prove an intervention effect unless that change is significantly different from the placebo group’s change over the same period. In a randomized longitudinal trial, conclusions about efficacy require a between-group comparison of change, ideally across all time points. Since the paper’s claims of dose-dependent and time-dependent benefit rest on invalid comparisons and misinterpreted significance testing, the conclusions that ACV caused weight loss and metabolic improvement are not supported.
correct_approach: The study should have been analyzed using methods appropriate for longitudinal randomized trial data: repeated-measures ANOVA, linear mixed-effects models, or ANCOVA/mixed models comparing post-treatment outcomes while adjusting for baseline values. The prespecified primary analysis should test the treatment-by-time interaction or compare change-from-baseline between each ACV dose group and placebo, with correction for multiple comparisons across outcomes, doses, and time points. An intention-to-treat framework, transparent handling of missing data, and reporting of effect sizes with confidence intervals should also have been used. In short, the correct approach is to model within-subject correlation and directly test whether ACV groups improved more than placebo.
additional_errors: Multiple outcomes and multiple time-point comparisons were tested without any clear multiplicity correction, increasing false-positive risk.
The manuscript reports only p-values/significance markers, with no confidence intervals or clear effect-estimation framework.
The placebo composition (lactic acid in water) may not be biologically inert and could complicate interpretation.
Convenience sampling limits generalizability despite randomization after enrollment.
No clear CONSORT-style reporting of attrition, protocol deviations, or missing-data handling is provided.
Baseline fasting glucose values are unusually high for a cohort described as having no chronic disease, raising concerns about sample characterization/data quality.
The description of blinding and randomization is sparse and does not clearly document allocation concealment or who generated and implemented randomization.
Using numerous pairwise week-by-week tests to claim dose dependence is inferior to a single model testing trend/dose-response across groups and time.
